---
title: "How Indian packaged food labels write additive numbers — and what that means for any parser hitting label text"
date: 2026-03-26
categories: [ifid, caas, label-conventions, data-analysis, track-a]
author:
  - "Lalitha A R"
  - "Claude (Anthropic)"
description: "E-numbers and INS numbers refer to the same additives. On Indian labels, both appear — sometimes in the same product category, sometimes in the same ingredient string. This report documents the notation variants found in 1,026 raw label strings and states what that means for IFID's ingredient matching design."
format:
  html:
    toc: true
---

## The question

IFID's additives taxonomy is standardised to INS numbers. The CAAS raw label data
is whatever Indian manufacturers printed on the pack.

The question is not whether there is mixing. The question is: what exactly does the
mixing look like, and what are the distinct notation variants a parser must handle
before it can do taxonomy lookup?

---

## Source

All findings in this report are from `caas/core/sampling/unified_ingredients_data-raw_updated.json`:
1,026 product rows, each carrying `sku_name` and `exact_ingredients`. The dataset
covers branded Indian packaged food products sourced from OpenFoodFacts; it was
filtered and deduplicated prior to this analysis. See `caas/claims/offdata_claims.md`
for the filtering decisions.

---

## What the data shows

### E-numbers and INS numbers both appear

88 products in the 1,026-row dataset declare at least one additive using an EU
E-number (`E{n}` format). 113 products declare at least one additive using an INS
number (`INS {n}` or `Ins {n}` format). The two sets overlap: 35 distinct additive
numbers appear in both E-number and INS-number form across the dataset — the same
additive, two naming conventions, different products.

The 46 distinct E-numbers found in the dataset:

`E100, E102, E110, E120, E122, E124, E133, E1422, E1442, E200, E202, E211, E260,
E282, E296, E319, E322, E330, E331, E339, E341, E407, E410, E412, E415, E417,
E418, E435, E440, E442, E446, E452, E4521, E466, E471, E475, E476, E492, E500,
E501, E508, E521, E551, E627, E631, E635, E960`

E-numbers in this dataset come primarily from chocolate and confectionery products
(Amul, Cadbury lines) and from masala snack and noodle products. INS numbers appear
more broadly, including in snack foods, dairy, and beverages.

### The same additive, both conventions

Selected examples where the same INS/E number appears under both conventions across
different products:

| Additive | E-number form | Products using E-form | INS-number form | Products using INS-form |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Lecithin | E322 | amul belgian milk chocolate, amul camel milk chocolate | INS 322 | tata soulfull ragi bites, havmor ice cream choco brownie |
| Disodium guanylate | E631 | kurkure zig zag, ching's secret paneer chilli masala | INS 631 | bingo potato chips, yippee 2-minute masala noodles |
| Polyglycerol polyricinoleate | E476 | amul belgian milk chocolate | INS 476 | sugar free d'lite sweetener |
| Silicon dioxide | E551 | ching's secret instant noodles manchurian | INS 551 | bingo potato chips, candyman tadka lollipop |
| Citric acid | E330 | haldiram's sabudana tikki, kwality wall's cassata | INS 330 | bingo potato chips, doritos sweet chili, kurkure puffcorn |

The pattern is not random. E-numbers appear on products from manufacturers who also
sell into European markets or follow European labeling conventions. INS numbers follow
FSSAI conventions for the Indian market.

### Four distinct notation variants

The dataset shows four distinct ways additive numbers appear in ingredient strings:

**Variant 1: `E{n}` — EU E-number, no prefix space**

```
E322, E476, E631, E551, E330
```

Appears in strings from chocolate and snack products following European convention.
No space between prefix and number.

**Variant 2: `INS {n}` — all-caps prefix, space, number**

```
INS 330, INS 551, INS 471, INS 102
```

The most common INS form. 77 products in the dataset use this variant. Appears in
all product categories.

**Variant 3: `Ins {n}` — mixed-case prefix, space, number**

```
Ins 330, Ins 296, Ins 551, Ins 300
```

25 products use this variant. Functionally identical to Variant 2 but the case
difference will cause a case-sensitive string match to fail. `INS 551` and `Ins 551`
are the same substance; no parser should treat them differently.

**Variant 4: Bare number in parentheses — no prefix**

```
Colour (162), Citric Acid (330), Acidity Regulator (330),
Anticaking Agent (551), Stabilizer (170)
```

The number appears in parentheses after the functional class or common name, with
no `INS` or `E` prefix. Examples found in Doritos, Kurkure, and Lay's ingredient
strings. The number is unambiguous only if the reader knows it is an INS number
— the string gives no explicit signal.

A fifth structural variant also appears — the number inside a parenthetical that
already contains the functional class declaration:

```
Acidity Regulators (Ins 296, Ins 330, Ins 334)
(INS 296), (Ins 551), (INS 508)
```

This is a punctuation pattern, not a new numbering convention — the number is still
Variant 2 or 3 — but a parser that splits on parentheses or commas first may not
extract the numbers cleanly without handling this structure.

### What was not found

No US FD&C naming was found in this dataset. "Red 40," "Yellow 6," "Blue 1" and
similar US convention names do not appear. The convention mixing in Indian label
data is specifically EU E-number vs Indian INS-number. US naming is not a third
variant to handle.

---

## Design implication for IFID

IFID's additives taxonomy uses INS numbers as keys. Any pipeline that takes Indian
label text and looks up an ingredient against the taxonomy must normalise additive
numbers before lookup.

The normalisation step needs to handle at minimum:

1. Strip `E` prefix, match remaining digits to INS key
2. Strip `INS ` prefix (all caps, with space) to get digits
3. Strip `Ins ` prefix (mixed case, with space) to get digits
4. Recognise bare parenthetical numbers `(\d{3,4})` as INS numbers when adjacent
   to a functional class or common name

Steps 1–3 require a case-insensitive match or explicit case normalisation. Step 4
requires context — the bare number is structurally ambiguous without the adjacent
text.

The functional class parenthetical pattern `Acidity Regulators (Ins 296, Ins 330,
Ins 334)` requires the parser to extract multiple numbers from within a single
parenthetical, not one number per parenthetical.

These are not edge cases. Variants 2 and 3 together cover 102 of the 113 products
with INS notation; the bare-number variant appears across at least 8 distinct
products from three major snack brands.

---

## Hindi language coverage in the source data

`caas/claims/offdata_claims.md` Claim 05 documents the following for the full
19,748-row OpenFoodFacts export used as the CAAS source:

- `product_name_hi`: 111 non-null rows (0.56% of 19,748)
- `ingredients_text_hi`: 11 non-null rows (0.056% of 19,748)
- `generic_name_hi`: 2 non-null rows (0.010% of 19,748)

The 4,104-row filtered working dataset used for the ingredient string analysis above
inherits this coverage profile. There are 11 rows at most with Hindi ingredient text
in the entire filtered dataset.

OpenFoodFacts cannot serve as a source of Hindi-language ingredient data for Indian
products. The dataset was structured and filtered for English-language coverage;
the Hindi fields are populated only incidentally by contributors.

Any IFID work that requires Hindi ingredient text — for matching against regional
product declarations, for labeling analysis of products that carry only Hindi text,
or for coverage of products not represented in English — requires a different data
source than this OFF export.

---

## Data files

| File | Role |
|---|---|
| `caas/core/sampling/unified_ingredients_data-raw_updated.json` | 1,026 product rows; source for all notation counts and examples in this report |
| `caas/claims/offdata_claims.md` | 7 verified claims on the full 19,748-row OFF export; Hindi coverage figures sourced from Claim 05 |

---

## Note on AI authorship

This document was drafted by Claude (Anthropic, Sonnet 4.6) in a Claude Code session
on 2026-03-26, from a structured plan written by Lalitha A R.

**What Claude did:**

- Ran pattern searches across all 1,026 product rows to count E-number and
  INS-number occurrences, identify notation variants, and find specific product
  examples for each variant
- Verified the 35-number overlap between E and INS conventions by matching digit
  sequences across both pattern sets
- Confirmed the four notation variants from the raw data, not from the plan
- Drafted all prose, tables, and section structure

**Constraints Claude worked under:**

- No claims were made about what patterns exist without first verifying them in
  the source file. The plan described what was expected; this report states what
  was found.
- Counts (88 products, 113 products, 77 products, 25 products, 46 E-numbers) are
  from the analysis run, not from the plan.
- The US FD&C absence is documented as a negative finding, not an assumption.
- Hindi coverage figures are from `caas/claims/offdata_claims.md` Claim 05 — a
  verified claim from a prior session — not from this session's analysis.
